text:
# Page 1^page=1
Veriﬁable and Reproducible LLM-Based Extraction of AAS Constraints from IDTA Submodel Template PDFs Mohammad Hadi Khori Jannatabadi*, Sebastian Heppner, Torben Miny, Tobias Kleinert Chair of Information and Automation Systems RWTH Aachen University Aachen, Germany {h.jannatabadi, s.heppner, t.miny, kleinert}@iat.rwth-aachen.de Abstract—Industrie 4.0 increasingly relies on the Asset Ad- ministration Shell (AAS) as a standards-based path to digital twins. The European Digital Product Passport (DPP), mandated by Regulation (EU) 2024/1781, may use diverse data carriers, and the AAS information model has been proposed as one candidate carrier. An AAS consists of Submodels that describe speciﬁc aspects of an asset. Each Submodel can be standard- ised as a Submodel Template (SMT), e.g., by the Industrial Digital Twin Association (IDTA), and the SMT ﬁxes the class hierarchy and the semantic identiﬁers that an AAS Submodel instance must populate. Each published SMT consists of machine- interpretable AASX and JSON ﬁles alongside a human-readable speciﬁcation PDF, and a subset of constraints (prose rules, conditional cardinality, units, dictionary references) appears only in the PDF. Lifting these PDF-anchored constraints into machine- interpretable records is a prerequisite for populating Submodel instances downstream. This paper presents an extraction protocol in which a Large Language Model (LLM) reads layout-aware blocks of the speci- ﬁcation PDF and proposes typed records. An automated veriﬁer then accepts a record only when its cited PDF block resolves and its emitted value matches the expected shape. This record-level veriﬁcation is distinct from checking a generated AAS Submodel instance against an SMT. The protocol is evaluated on three IDTA SMTs: the Dig- ital Nameplate (IDTA 02006 v3.0.1), the Generic Frame for Technical Data (IDTA 02003 v1.2), and the Battery Nameplate (IDTA 02035-1 v1.0). Across ﬁve repeated runs on all three SMTs, the protocol produced bit-identical output, with ﬁeld-level F1 averaging approximately 0.64 (out of 1.0). Recall, prompt sensitivity, and cross-SMT portability remain the main open barriers. Index Terms—Asset Administration Shell, Submodel Template, Large Language Models, schema-aware extraction, constraint veriﬁcation, reproducibility, abstention vocabulary I. INTRODUCTION Industrie 4.0 increasingly relies on standards-based digital twins to enable cross-vendor interoperability, with the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) as an implementation path [1]. Regulatory pressure has accelerated this trajectory. Regulation (EU) 2024/1781 establishes the framework under which ap- plicable delegated acts can require a Digital Product Passport (DPP) for covered products placed on the European Union market, and includes explicit provisions for interoperable, machine-readable data carriers [2, Art. 10(1)(c),(d)]. The AAS information model has been proposed as one candidate carrier for the DPP in recent multi-stakeholder work [3]. Within that information model, an Industrial Digital Twin Associa- tion (IDTA) Submodel Template (SMT) ﬁxes the structural class hierarchy and the semantic identiﬁers an instance must populate, with the Digital Nameplate (02006 v3.0.1) [4] and the Generic Frame for Technical Data (02003 v1.2) [5] acting as the anchor SMTs. Before AAS Submodel instances conforming to an SMT can be generated automatically from manufacturer data, the constraints that deﬁne conformance must be available in a machine-interpretable form. Each published IDTA SMT consists of two artefact classes. The machine-interpretable AASX and JSON ﬁles encode the SMT’s class structure, value types, cardinality bounds, and semantic identiﬁer references. The accompanying speciﬁcation PDF is human-readable. PDF- only constraints, present in the speciﬁcation PDF and not captured by the AASX or JSON ﬁles, include prose rules, conditional cardinality, unit constraints, and dictionary ref- erences [4], [5]. End-to-end automation of SMT instantia- tion faces multiple obstacles, including value extraction from weakly-structured manufacturer datasheets that recent AAS- Large Language Model (LLM) work targets as the primary content source [6]. The contribution presented here addresses one upstream obstacle in this chain: lifting the PDF-only constraints into machine-interpretable records that downstream Submodel-instance population can later check against. An LLM reads layout-aware blocks of the speciﬁcation PDF and proposes typed records. Each record names a position in the SMT (an AAS path), a constraint type drawn from a deﬁned set, and a value drawn from a ﬁxed vocabulary, and explicitly cites the source block from which it was read. An automated veriﬁer accepts a record only when the cited block resolves, the constraint type is one of the deﬁned values, and the emitted value matches the expected shape. Records that fail any check are excluded from the output. No human intervention is required at runtime. This record- level veriﬁcation is distinct from checking a generated AAS Submodel instance against an SMT.

# Page 2^page=2
The corresponding research question is: Can an LLM- supported pipeline extract machine-interpretable constraints from SMT speciﬁcation PDFs in a veriﬁable and reproducible way? The main contributions of this work are: 1) an end-to-end constraint-extraction protocol in which the LLM is conﬁned to a ﬁxed value vocabulary and SMT positions, every emitted record cites the source PDF block from which it was read, and an automated veriﬁer is the sole acceptance layer. 2) an empirical demonstration on the Digital Nameplate that the protocol produces bit-identical output across ﬁve repeated runs at a ﬁxed prompt, model, and hardware conﬁguration, establishing practical output stability un- der these conditions. 3) a cross-SMT evaluation pairing end-to-end extraction on three IDTA SMTs: the Digital Nameplate, the Generic Frame for Technical Data, and the Battery Nameplate (IDTA 02035-1 v1.0) [7], with structural validation of the underlying bounded-graph walker across all 131 published IDTA SMTs. The remainder of this work is structured as follows. Sec- tion II surveys the AAS information model, LLM-based information extraction, and existing AAS veriﬁcation tools. Section III presents the constraint-extraction concept. Sec- tion IV describes the implementation. Section V reports the cross-SMT evaluation and discusses limitations. Section VI concludes. II. RELATED WORK A. Asset Administration Shell The AAS metamodel [1] deﬁnes a class hierarchy of Asse- tAdministrationShell, Submodel, SubmodelElement and Prop- erty, where Submodels and SubmodelElements may carry a semanticId reference and the ModellingKind enumeration distinguishes type-level Submodel templates from instance- level Submodels. An SMT ﬁxes idShorts, multiplicities, qualiﬁers, and property data types for a class of instances. An AAS Submodel instance conforming to a given SMT therefore differs primarily by concrete values and selected optional elements [8]. An IDTA SMT can be read either as a copy template or as an information-model type, and only the second reading provides the constraints needed to verify that an instance is faithful to its SMT. The AAS meta- model and its normative JSON Schema deﬁne the structural and qualiﬁer-level semantics that both the JSON and AASX serialisations preserve, including the Qualiﬁer class and the TemplateQualiﬁer kind used to attach SMT-level cardinality at the SubmodelElement level [1]. For IDTA 02006 Digital Nameplate, the published JSON SMT ﬁle encodes cardinality, value types, and semantic identiﬁer references, but some prose, conditional-cardinality, unit, and dictionary constraints are only recoverable from the accompanying PDF [4]. B. Large Language Models Modern LLMs are applied to converting unstructured text into structured records [9]. Prompt design has matured into a systematised ﬁeld, originating with in-context few-shot prompting [10] and consolidated in surveys [11]. The tem- perature parameter controls output variation, with temperature zero requesting the most conservative decoding the inference runtime allows. Greedy decoding selects the most likely next token at each step. Three principal shortcomings are pertinent to this work. Hallucination is a central failure mode for structured and long-form generation [12], [13]. Prompt sensi- tivity has been quantiﬁed empirically: small wording changes can produce substantially different extracted records [14]. Stochasticity persists even at temperature zero with greedy decoding and a ﬁxed seed, with runtime-dependent accuracy gaps of up to ﬁfteen percent reported in speciﬁc empirical settings [15], [16]. C. AAS Veriﬁcation Tools Three categories of tooling verify AAS artefacts. The aas-test-engines project [17] veriﬁes the structural conformance of an AAS ﬁle in JSON, XML, or AASX form against the metamodel schemas of [1] and the con- formance of an AAS server to the API speciﬁcation. The compliance_tool of the BaSyx-Python SDK [18] per- forms syntactic deserialisation against the ofﬁcial schemas and structural validation, with narrower scope but additional verbosity. SDK-level and reactive testing approaches [19], [20] extend conformance to runtime behaviour against the API speciﬁcation. All three categories operate on already-built AAS ﬁles or running AAS servers. Beyond veriﬁcation, code- gen tools such as aas-submodel-template-to-py [21] generate typed Python classes from IDTA SMTs, targeting object-oriented instance construction rather than constraint extraction. AAS-LLM work targets instance generation from technical text with proprietary GPT-class models [6], and no prior work in this review veriﬁes records prior to instantiation against an SMT-derived constraint graph. D. Veriﬁable and Reproducible LLM Outputs Schema-conformant output is the prerequisite for machine- readable extraction. JSON Schema [22] provides the structural target, and constrained-decoding engines such as Outlines [23], Microsoft Guidance [24], and lm-format-enforcer [25] enforce schema conformance by masking invalid tokens during generation. Enforcing that the LLM output conforms to a schema can degrade the quality of the generated content [26], and benchmark work documents low coverage of real-world schemas across these engines [27]. Schema conformance therefore guarantees that the output parses, not that the content is correct, which motivates post-hoc veriﬁcation of generated records. Post-hoc veriﬁcation of LLM output has been studied in two main forms. Iterative self-reﬁnement loops, represented by Reﬂexion [28] and CRITIC [29], feed veriﬁer critiques back to the LLM and request a rewrite. Compiled-pipeline

# Page 3^page=3
soft-and-hard assertions, represented by DSPy Assertions [30] within the broader DSPy framework [31], embed structural constraints inside the prompt-and-program ﬂow. A related line of work grounds the prompt in a bounded set of valid output targets. Schema-anchored extraction with annotation guide- lines (GoLLIE [32]) restricts emission to a ﬁnite ontology, and KnowGPT [33] prompts the LLM with subgraph paths from an external knowledge graph. Bounded-graph anchoring against a closed set, under the closed-world assumption [34], treats any absent path as a hallucination. Another line of work treats each generated record as an atomic claim requiring source attribution. FActScore [35] decomposes long-form output into atomic facts, SAFE [36] veriﬁes each through a separate retrieval step, and ALCE [37] benchmarks citation quality. Self-RAG [38] couples retrieval grounding with reﬂective abstention via reﬂection tokens. When a record cannot be supported, abstention is the ap- propriate response, and surveys taxonomise abstention into multiple status classes [39], [40]. Reproducibility of LLM- based pipelines is supported by the NeurIPS reproducibility checklist [41] and empirical Natural Language Processing (NLP) reporting practice [42]. III. CONCEPT The proposed protocol takes the IDTA SMT speciﬁcation PDF as input and emits veriﬁed constraint records. The LLM proposes one record at a time, and a deterministic veriﬁer accepts the record only when its cited PDF block resolves and the emitted value is drawn from the ﬁxed vocabulary (Section III-C lists the full check set). No human intervention is required at runtime. Fig. 1 depicts the protocol. The next four subsections describe its components: how the PDF is parsed into evidence blocks, how the LLM generates typed records against a bounded template graph, how each record is veriﬁed before acceptance, and how the entire run is reproducible from a single run manifest. A. Parsing the PDF A rule-based layout extractor converts the source PDF into a stream of typed evidence blocks. An evidence block is a rect- angular region of the page identiﬁed by SMT number, zero- padded page, and block index, e.g., smt02006_p008_b006 as shown in Fig. 1(b). The extractor distinguishes two block types: rows of the SMT element tables, which carry the structured per-element data, and the prose paragraphs that sur- round them, which carry contextual rules. Each block carries a unique identiﬁer that downstream records cite. The veriﬁer conﬁrms that the LLM did not hallucinate the cited block by checking that the cited identiﬁer resolves to an existing PDF block (Section III-C, Check (5)). The extractor is deterministic, and its output and Secure Hash Algorithm (SHA)-256 enter the run manifest as one of ﬁve inputs identiﬁed in Section III-D. B. Generation of a Machine-Readable Format The bounded template node graph (Fig. 1(c)) is the pro- tocol’s central artefact. In this paper, AAS path denotes the absolute idShort path in the template graph. Each node may also carry a semantic identiﬁer reference, an Internationalized Resource Identiﬁer (IRI) [43] or an International Registration Data Identiﬁer (IRDI) [44], that identiﬁes the semantic deﬁ- nition of the addressed node for downstream consumers. The graph enumerates every AAS path that the LLM is allowed to emit. Any path absent from the graph is treated as a hallucination under the closed-world assumption [34]. The graph is built by a rule-based walker that visits each node of the IDTA JSON SMT ﬁle and labels it with its full AAS path. The LLM input is the concatenation of three artefacts (Fig. 1(d)): the canonical record JSON schema, the bounded template graph, and the current evidence block. The LLM emits zero or more typed records per evidence block. Each record ﬁlls the canonical schema with the SMT identiﬁer and version, the AAS path, the constraint class and value, the formalization status, the source evidence identiﬁers, and provenance metadata. Each emitted record carries two controlled vocabularies: a constraint class describing what the record encodes, and a formalization status describing how the LLM read the source. The constraint class takes one of six values, determined by analysis of IDTA-published SMTs: value_type, cardinality, sme_type, semantic_identity, format, or structural_containment. The formalization status forms a closed status vocabulary of ﬁve values: exact when the source states the value or rule directly and unambiguously, approximate when the meaning is clear but the ﬁxed vocabulary loses nuance, ambiguous when more than one reading is admissible at the block, prose_only when the source states a rule in prose that does not ﬁt the current schema, and requires_external_resolution when the rule depends on an external resource such as ECLASS or IEC CDD. A non-exact status is a deliberate signal that the source did not support a direct answer. C. Automated Veriﬁcation of LLM Output The veriﬁer accepts the record only when all seven checks pass. The checks are organised into three phases and run in ﬁxed order. The ﬁrst failure rejects the record. The Syntactic phase checks the record’s output syntax, status values, and canonical form. Check (1) schema-valid asserts that the record matches the canonical JSON schema. Check (2) status in vocabulary asserts that formalization_status lies in the closed status vocabulary. Check (3) value canonical asserts that constraint_value matches the canonical form for its class. The Referential phase performs the protocol’s hallucina- tion detection. Check (4) node in bounded graph requires aas_path to exist in the graph. A record whose path is absent is rejected as out-of-graph under the bounded graph check, because the graph enumerates every valid tem- plate node. Check (5) evidence ID resolves requires ev- ery cited evidence_id to resolve to an existing block.

˜ (b) Evidence blocks Page-anchored typed blocks; example ID: smt02006_p008_b006 K (a) Source artefacts Pinned IDTA SMT spec: PDF + JSON template ™ (c) Bounded template node graph AAS-path-addressed nodes; examples: /Nameplate/URIOfTheProduct /Nameplate/Markings/*/MarkingName * (d) LLM LLM inference runtime ﬁxed inference conﬁg ? Schema-aware prompt schema + graph + evidence block ½ (e) Veriﬁer Accepted only when checks 1–7 pass: Syntactic (1) schema-valid (2) status in vocab. (3) value canonical Referential (4) node in bounded graph (5) evidence ID resolves (6) semantic-id family recognised Auditable (7) provenance recorded (f) Accepted records ¢ Outcome badges: schema-valid graph-resolved evidence-cited provenance-recorded status ∈ {exact, approximate, ambiguous, prose_only} (g) Rejected records S Failure labels: (1) schema_invalid (2) status_outside_vocabulary (3) value_not_canonical (4) node_hallucinated (5) evidence_id_unresolved (6) semantic_id_unknown (7) provenance_incomplete † requires_external_resolution ò (h) Veriﬁed extraction output Accepted records, eligible for downstream AAS rule generation after status ﬁlteringrule-based layout parser rule-based walker rejections revise prompt (development only; disabled during evaluation) Ã PARSE * GENERATE ½ VERIFY D OUTPUT Fig. 1: Veriﬁer-ﬁrst protocol for reproducible constraint extraction from IDTA Submodel Templates. (a) Source artefacts feed (b) page-anchored evidence blocks and (c) a bounded template node graph. (d) The LLM combines (b), (c), and the canonical record schema as a schema-aware prompt and emits typed records. (e) The veriﬁer accepts a record only if all seven checks pass across three phases (Syntactic, Referential, Auditable), routing it to (f) accepted records or (g) rejected records. Accepted records feed (h), the veriﬁed extraction output eligible for downstream Submodel-instance validation. The dashed arrow is the prompt-evolution feedback path. Check (6) semantic-id family recognised asserts that when a semanticId is present on the graph node, it con- forms to an IRI [43] or an IRDI [44], per the AAS meta- model [1]. The IRDI is accepted in two written forms, the slash form (e.g., 0112/2///N#X#N) and the dash form (e.g., 0173-1#02-...). The Auditable phase has one check. Check (7) provenance recorded requires the record to carry prompt_version, prompt_sha256, model, temperature, and identification_run_id. An accepted record (Fig. 1(f)) is tagged with the four success markers from Checks (1), (4), (5), and (7), and with a status drawn from {exact, approximate, ambiguous, prose_only}. A rejected record (Fig. 1(g)) is tagged with the failing check’s numeral and a machine- readable failure label such as schema_invalid or node_hallucinated. A record whose status is requires_external_resolution is marked with the dagger glyph in Fig. 1(g), reﬂecting that the constraint cannot be ﬁnalised inside the protocol without an external resource. Rejected records are fed back to the LLM only during the prompt-development phase and never during evaluation. This split is enforced by the run manifest. D. Reproducibility A single run manifest ﬁxes ﬁve inputs to the protocol: (i) the pinned IDTA commit, (ii) the pre-registered scoring scope, (iii) the SHA-256 hashes of the evidence corpus and the bounded template graph, (iv) the prompt-SHA (the SHA-256 of the rendered prompt text), and (v) the model identiﬁer and inference conﬁguration. Given the same ﬁve inputs, a re-run is designed to produce the same accept-and-reject decisions on the same evidence blocks. This property is called practical output stability. The manifest ﬁxes the conditions under which it is measured. IV. IMPLEMENTATION The implementation realises the protocol as a Python pipeline. The layout extractor uses pymupdf1 to convert the source PDF into the typed evidence blocks of Section III-A. The bounded template node graph walker assigns each IDTA SMT node to its absolute AAS path. The walker is validated against the entire 131-template IDTA SMT catalogue, cover- ing 11,124 nodes, and every node resolves to a recognised AAS Submodel element type. The implementation is publicly archived 2. The LLM is served by Ollama [45] running qwen2.5:7b-instruct [46] at temperature 0.0 on an Apple M4 Max. An open-weight model is chosen to support reproducibility. A. Pipeline Architecture The per-run outputs are split into two zones. The automatic- protocol zone holds artefacts the protocol determines without reference to gold or scoring. The evaluation-overlay zone holds artefacts used only for evaluation. The automatic- protocol zone covers six stages: the source PDF and run man- ifest, the typed evidence blocks of Section III-A, the bounded template node graph of Section III-B, the ﬁve prompt versions 1Available at https://pymupdf.readthedocs.io. 2Available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20127614.

# Page 5^page=5
of Section IV-B, the per-record LLM responses, and the canon- ical records on which the veriﬁer acts. The evaluation-overlay zone covers ﬁve further stages reported in Section V: the v4 example pool, the gold constraints, the scoring outputs, the bounded-section coverage, and the veriﬁer-delta and stability diagnostics. The seven veriﬁer checks of Section III-C are implemented in the runner module’s Verifier class. B. Prompt Evolution Five prompt versions, denoted v0 through v4, were au- thored in sequence. The base v0–v4 set is archived on Zen- odo3. The v4-portable variant used for the IDTA 02035-1 evaluation is pre-materialised per target template. All ﬁve versions share a baseline of atomicity, canonical values, and abstention vocabulary. v1 adds layout-aware reading rules that anchor each extracted value to a speciﬁc page-anchored layout block, together with strict evidence citation. v2 adds the bounded template graph and requires path-addressed output. v3 adds the honesty contract, which directs the LLM to emit approximate, ambiguous, or prose_only when the cited evidence does not support an exact value. v4 adds nine worked examples. The additive structural relationship between versions is summarised alongside the empirical comparison in Table I of Section V-D. The ﬁve versions are reported as a series because Sec- tion V-D evaluates each version’s performance separately. C. Reproducibility Artefacts Every run writes a run manifest that records the ﬁve reproducibility inputs of Section III-D (the IDTA commit, the pre-registered scoring scope, the corpus and template-graph hashes, the prompt-SHA, and the model and inference conﬁg- uration), alongside a human-readable summary for debugging and error analysis. V. EVALUATION The protocol reaches an average F1 of approximately 0.64 across three IDTA SMTs (precision 0.74, recall 0.59), with v4 leading on all three. Output is bit-identical across ﬁve repeated runs at ﬁxed prompt and inference conﬁguration. Cross-SMT transfer is uneven. The held-out IDTA 02035-1 v1.0 SMT requires bounded-graph anchoring: prompt versions without it (v0 and v1) yield F1 = 0.000 on this SMT (Section V-B). A deterministic regex baseline beats v4 by 0.115 F1 on IDTA 02006 but does not transport across SMTs. A. Setup IDTA 02006 v3.0.1 [4] is the primary evaluation target. A manual gold set of 58 records covers the ﬁelds the Digital Nameplate requires at its top level. For the held-out cross- SMT extension, the gold sets are 22 records on IDTA 02003 v1.2 [5] (manually annotated) and 60 records on IDTA 02035- 1 v1.0 [7] (extracted by the walker and veriﬁed against the source PDF). Across all three SMTs, the LLM is shown only 3Available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20128130. TABLE I: Additive prompt-version feature matrix. A high- lighted cell marks the ﬁrst version that carries a feature, and all later versions carry it as well. Column v4-p is the v4- portable cross-template variant of v4, which replaces v4’s IDTA 02006 worked examples with template-speciﬁc exam- ples. Read alongside Table II for per-version ﬁeld-level results. Prompt version Feature v0 v1 v2 v3 v4 v4-p baseline ■ ■ ■ ■ ■ ■ layout-aware reading rules – ■ ■ ■ ■ ■ strict evidence citation – ■ ■ ■ ■ ■ bounded template graph – – ■ ■ ■ ■ honesty contract – – – ■ ■ ■ nine 02006 examples – – – – ■ – nine per-template examples – – – – – ■ the evidence blocks that contain the gold-set ﬁelds, an in- scope feeding regime applied uniformly across SMTs. Each result reports precision P = TP/(TP + FP), recall R = TP/(TP + FN), and the F1 score F1 = 2P R/(P + R). Among the ﬁve prompt versions, v4 prepends nine worked examples to the prompt. Because ﬁve of these examples are drawn from IDTA 02006, v4’s F1 on 02006 is an upper bound rather than a held-out result. On 02003 and 02035-1, the example identiﬁers do not overlap with the gold. B. Cross-Template Transfer The walker and veriﬁer are portable across IDTA JSON SMTs. Empirical evidence here covers the three reported SMTs. On the held-out IDTA 02035-1 v1.0 SMT, graph-blind v0 and v1 collapse to F1 = 0.000 because the LLM emits camel-cased idShort tokens that do not resolve against the bounded template graph (Table II(c)). Graph-anchored v2 through v4 recover non-zero F1 on the same SMT. Cross-SMT portability of the protocol therefore requires the bounded graph in addition to the LLM. C. Practical Output Stability On IDTA 02006, each of the ﬁve prompt versions emits identical (AAS path, constraint class, constraint value) records across its ﬁve repeated runs (75 distinct records in total, zero variation across repeats). The same bit-identical pattern holds on 02003 and 02035-1 (Table II). The ﬁve-repeat choice fol- lows reproducibility methodology that recommends repeated- run variance reporting [41], [42]. Practical output stability at this conﬁguration means downstream Submodel-instance validation can build on the same record set across re-runs. D. Prompt-Version Comparison The v0–v4 trajectory on IDTA 02006 (Table II, with the additive feature structure in Table I) shows three measurable effects. Adding layout-aware reading rules (v1) lifts F1 from 0.453 to 0.635 at P = 1.000 by correctly emitting 18 cardi- nality records that v0 missed. Adding the honesty contract

# Page 6^page=6
(v3) preserves P = 1.000 at F1 = 0.602 under bounded- graph anchoring. Adding worked examples (v4) raises recall to 0.776 and F1 to 0.732, with the corresponding precision drop concentrated in cardinality (16 of 20 FPs). With three SMTs, the v0–v4 comparison alone cannot fully separate prompt-strategy from the example-pool and evidence-feeding- protocol effects bounded by the ablation in Section V-G. E. Ablation: Deterministic Regex Baseline As an ablation, a deterministic regex baseline is hand- derived for the IDTA 02006 layout and run on the same eight scoring blocks. The baseline emits one record per IDTA element-table row whose layout matches a ﬁxed family of patterns (an idShort token, an aggregation marker such as Property, Submodel, or File, a cardinality cell, and an optional semanticId cell). It uses neither the LLM nor the bounded template graph. The regex baseline reaches F1 = 0.847 (precision 0.887, recall 0.810, 47 true positives out of 58 gold records). The best-F1 LLM version (v4) reaches F1 = 0.732, an absolute F1 gap of 0.115. The remaining gap to F1 = 1.000 reﬂects internal layout inconsistency within IDTA 02006 itself that a single ﬁxed-pattern family does not capture. The baseline does not transport to a second IDTA SMT. The layout patterns differ across SMTs, so any new SMT would require a fresh set of regexes hand-derived from that SMT’s speciﬁcation. The LLM-based protocol of Section III is structurally portable across the 131 IDTA SMTs on which the walker has been validated, with end-to-end extraction empir- ically evaluated on three SMTs. The ablation therefore quan- tiﬁes the F1 cost of choosing structurally-portable extraction over SMT-speciﬁc extraction on a compact-Uniﬁed Modeling Language (UML) evidence layout. Automated derivation of per-SMT regex from each IDTA speciﬁcation is discussed as a research direction in Section VI-B. F. False-Negative Decomposition Across ﬁve repeated runs each of v2 (P = 0.950, F1 = 0.487) and v3 (P = 1.000, F1 = 0.602), every false negative on the 58-record IDTA 02006 gold falls into a single diagnostic bucket: the pair (AAS path, constraint class) never appears in any LLM emission. None arise from value mismatch or calibrated abstention. The dominant missed class for v2 and v3 is cardinality (23 of 39 v2 FNs, 59%), with both versions emitting zero cardinality records. v1 closes this gap without graph anchoring (18 cardinality TPs, zero FPs), and v4 closes it under graph anchoring via worked-example cardinality recov- ery (13 TPs at 16 FPs). On v4 applied to IDTA 02035-1 v1.0, 26 of 34 FNs (76%) fall in semantic_identity (13) and structural_containment (13), two graph- derivable classes the walker emits canonically. The remain- ing four classes reach recall ≈ 0.76, comparable to v4 on IDTA 02006. G. Ablation: Protocol and Example-Pool v4’s worked-example pool and the evidence-feeding regime are two design choices that confound the v0–v4 prompt- strategy series. Varying both independently isolates their con- tributions. Two evidence-feeding protocols (in-scope, feeding only scoring-scope blocks, versus all-blocks, feeding the full PDF) and three worked-example pools (a default plus two SMT-matched alternates) yield a 12-cell factorial design, run at K = 5 (60 runs). The evidence-feeding protocol is the dominant single vari- able. Switching from in-scope to all-blocks costs between 0.296 and 0.750 F1 on ﬁve of the six (pool, target) cells (one cell improves by 0.089). The worked-example pool effect is smaller and direction-sensitive. Under the in-scope protocol, the SMT-matched pool lifts F1 by 0.128 on IDTA 02006 and by 0.044 on IDTA 02003. The mismatched pool reduces F1 by 0.121 on IDTA 02006 and by 0.039 on IDTA 02003. The evidence-feeding regime is therefore the dominant single design lever for F1, substantially larger than the prompt- version effect. H. Threats to Validity Eight threats to validity bound the evaluation. First, dif- ferent gold annotation may shift the F1 gap between the LLM-based protocol and the regex baseline reported in Sec- tion V-E. Second, the ﬁve-run stability holds for the single (model, hardware, prompt-SHA) tuple reported. Other conﬁg- urations may diverge [15]. Third, the cross-SMT gold sets are small, so the current ﬁndings indicate that the protocol trans- fers, and larger gold sets may revise the magnitudes. Fourth, the only consistency check on the gold annotation has used synthetic perturbations, and a second human reviewer is future work. Fifth, the cross-SMT comparison runs the same v0– v3 base prompt-SHAs and v4 (with the pre-materialised v4- portable on 02035-1) under a single harmonised protocol, so the 12-cell factorial of Section V-G does not separate prompt- strategy from surface-form contributions at three SMTs. Sixth, veriﬁer check (5) conﬁrms that every cited evidence_id resolves to an existing block but does not check that the cited block entails the extracted value. Seventh, the recall ceiling reﬂects the deployed 7B-instruct model from Section V-F, so a larger model may improve recall. Eighth, the IDTA 02003 reference annotations are frozen against the v1.2 speciﬁcation used at pre-registration time. A successor v2.0 was released in March 2025, so cross-SMT results on IDTA 02003 are bounded by the v1.2 surface and may revise against v2.0. VI. CONCLUSION This paper presented a veriﬁer-ﬁrst protocol that lifts machine-interpretable constraints out of IDTA SMT speci- ﬁcation PDFs. The protocol parses each speciﬁcation PDF into page-anchored evidence blocks and restricts the LLM to a bounded template graph and a ﬁxed value vocabulary. It accepts a record only when an automated veriﬁer conﬁrms that its cited PDF block resolves and its emitted value matches

# Page 7^page=7
TABLE II: Per-SMT ﬁeld-level results for prompt versions v0–v4. Counts (TP, FP, FN) aggregate the ﬁve repeated runs. (a) IDTA 02006 v3.0.1 Digital Nameplate: 8 evidence blocks, 58 gold records∗ Version F1 P R TP FP FN v0 0.453 1.000 0.293 17 0 41 v1 0.635 1.000 0.466 27 0 31 v2 0.487 0.950 0.328 19 1 39 v3 0.602 1.000 0.431 25 0 33 v4 0.732 0.692 0.776 45 20 13 (b) IDTA 02003 v1.2 GeneralInformation: 39 evidence blocks, 22 gold records Version F1 P R TP FP FN v0 0.650 0.722 0.591 13 5 9 v1 0.516 0.889 0.364 8 1 14 v2 0.323 0.556 0.227 5 4 17 v3 0.483 1.000 0.318 7 0 15 v4 0.706 1.000 0.545 12 0 10 (c) IDTA 02035-1 v1.0 Battery Nameplate (Digital Battery Passport Part 1): 144 evidence blocks, 60 gold records Version F1 P R TP FP FN v0 0.000 0.000 0.000 0 0 60 v1 0.000 0.000 0.000 0 0 60 v2 0.247 0.476 0.167 10 11 50 v3 0.219 0.615 0.133 8 5 52 v4 0.473 0.520 0.433 26 24 34 ∗ v4 prepends nine worked examples (few-shot extraction demonstrations) to its prompt. Five of these overlap with records in the (a) IDTA 02006 gold set used for scoring, so the v4 row in (a) is an upper bound on v4’s generalisation to unseen records. the expected shape. Every run is reproducible from a single manifest that records the inputs, the model, and the prompt. Across ﬁve repeated runs on all three evaluated SMTs, the protocol produced bit-identical output, demonstrating practical output stability under ﬁxed inference settings. Field-level F1 averaged approximately 0.64 across the Digital Nameplate (IDTA 02006 v3.0.1), the Generic Frame for Technical Data (IDTA 02003 v1.2), and the Battery Nameplate (IDTA 02035- 1 v1.0). This stability is a measurable property of the protocol at a ﬁxed conﬁguration. A. Limitations Four conceptual limitations bound the protocol’s appli- cability. First, extraction quality is bounded by the qual- ity of the source IDTA SMT speciﬁcations. Ambiguous or inconsistent speciﬁcation PDFs propagate extraction errors into the output. Second, the protocol’s output is veriﬁed constraint records. Populating an AAS Submodel instance from these records is a separate downstream step. Third, constraint values are drawn from a closed six-class vocabulary (Section III-B). New constraint kinds appearing in future IDTA SMTs may require vocabulary extension. Fourth, the practical output stability property is measured for a single (Ollama, qwen2.5:7b-instruct, Apple M4 Max) conﬁguration. Portability of the stability property to other inference runtimes is unveriﬁed. B. Future Work Future work will broaden the manually annotated reference set to cover the full SMT surface, will re-measure stability on additional local models, and will add a second human reviewer of the reference annotations. A further direction is to derive a per-SMT regex automatically from each IDTA speciﬁcation as an additional intermediate format, yielding a deterministic runtime extractor motivated by the hand-derived baseline of Section V-E. The modest absolute F1 ceiling on IDTA 02006 (∼11 points above v4) and the non-transport across SMTs bound the gain this direction can deliver. Future work should also explore how this protocol can be used to populate AAS Submodel instances from manufacturer datasheet PDFs in a reproducible and veriﬁable way.
